title: Experiment Tracking
concept: Experiment tracking means recording what you trained, with which settings, on which data, and what happened, so model progress becomes a reliable learning process instead of a pile of guesses and screenshots.

exercises.prompt: Why do deep-learning teams track experiments instead of trusting memory?

options.text: Because many runs differ in subtle ways, and written records make results comparable.
Because model training never changes from run to run.
Because accuracy automatically explains where it came from.

exercises.explain: Exactly. Tracking turns fuzzy memories into evidence about what settings produced each result.

cards.title: Yesterday's best run disappears fast
cards.body: A team tries five learning rates, two batch sizes, and a different augmentation setting. By Friday, someone asks, “Which run gave the strong validation score?” If nobody logged the details, the answer becomes detective work instead of science.

cards.title: What experiment tracking really means
cards.body: Experiment tracking is the habit of saving the important facts about each run: the dataset version, model setup, hyperparameters, code state, and outcome metrics. The goal is simple: every result should be traceable back to the choices that created it.

cards.title: A lab notebook for neural networks
cards.body: Scientists do not run chemistry experiments and then scribble “something blue happened” on a sticky note. They keep a lab notebook. Experiment tracking plays the same role in deep learning: it records the recipe, the conditions, and the result so you can learn from each trial.

cards.title: What a tracked run might include
cards.body: Imagine a defect-detection project. One run logs: model = ResNet-18, learning rate = 0.001, batch size = 32, augmentation = horizontal flip, seed = 7, validation accuracy = 91%, and training time = 28 minutes. That single record lets the team compare fairly with the next run instead of relying on vague impressions.

cards.title: Accuracy alone is not enough
cards.body: A common myth is that the final score is the only thing worth saving. But one number without context is weak evidence. If you do not know the settings, data split, or code version behind a result, you cannot repeat it, explain it, or improve it confidently.

cards.title: Random notes vs. traceable runs
cards.body: Tracking changes model development from memory-based guessing to evidence-based iteration.

left.text:
Runs named final2_reallyfinal.
No clear record of hyperparameters.
Hard to explain why one result was better.

right.text:
Each run logs settings and metrics.
Results can be sorted and compared.
The best run is easy to trace and repeat.

exercises.prompt: Which detail is most important to save with a model score?

options.text: The hyperparameters and setup that produced the score.
Only the name of the laptop used that day.
A guess about why the score looks nice.
Nothing else, because metrics explain themselves.

exercises.explain: Right. A score becomes useful when it stays attached to the setup that produced it.

exercises.prompt: True or false: If two runs have the same accuracy, tracking still matters because their settings may differ.
exercises.explain: True. Two runs can land on the same score for different reasons, and those differences matter for future decisions.

exercises.sentence: Experiment tracking helps teams compare results instead of relying on ___.
exercises.explain: Memory is correct. The point is to replace fuzzy recall with a reliable record.

exercises.prompt: Which team is using experiment tracking well?

options.text: They log the dataset version, learning rate, seed, and validation score for each run.
They keep only the best screenshot and delete the settings.
They rename every run “best_model” and hope the meaning stays obvious.

exercises.explain: Exactly. Good tracking keeps the choices and the outcomes connected.

exercises.prompt: A teammate says, “Run 14 looked great, but I forgot whether it used a different batch size or a different augmentation.” What lesson is this team learning the hard way?

options.text: Experiment tracking matters because outcomes need to stay linked to the settings that produced them.
Tracking is unnecessary once one run looks promising.
Hyperparameters only matter before training begins.
The best fix is to stop comparing runs altogether.

exercises.explain: Exactly. A promising score is not very useful if the team cannot trace how it happened.
